discussion_comments.comment_body: You can't close a receiver channel, it needs to be closed by the sender. The goal of panicking here is to ensure that we don't ever leak channels and to ensure the app crashes if we are.

callback paradigm, thoughts? 
I thought this was a little less code than using a channel here 
and I think it works just as well because the callback should be a closure on the data that we need like the context and the cmd object etc

right?

I guess the down side is the execution of the StatDir function stops while the callback is being executed
I think that is okay for this purpose because the callback is just gonna write to the pty and then return

but maybe a channel would make it faster
I lock the entirety of AppendData, because append needs to get the offset for the end of the file and then update the end of the file without the end of the file changing

+package blockstore
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"encoding/json"
+	"fmt"

+
+	"github.com/alecthomas/units"
+)
+
+type FileOptsType struct {
+	MaxSize  int64
+	Circular bool
+	IJson    bool
+}
+
+type FileMeta = map[string]any
+
+type FileInfo struct {
+	BlockId   string
+	Name      string
+	Size      int64
+	CreatedTs int64
+	ModTs     int64
+	Opts      FileOptsType
+	Meta      FileMeta
+}
+
+const MaxBlockSize = int64(128 * units.Kilobyte)
+const DefaultFlushTimeout = 1 * time.Second
+
+type CacheEntry struct {
+	Lock       *sync.Mutex
+	CacheTs    int64
+	Info       *FileInfo
+	DataBlocks []*CacheBlock
+	Refs       int64

discussion_comments.comment_body: To fix a sync issue where flush cache would remove cache entry objects while write was writing to them, I added a refs parameter to the cache entry. Write increments it and flush can write file info and cache blocks to the db, but it can only remove a cache entry from the cache if refs is 0

you can't iterate directly over GlobalStore.Map (it needs to be synchronized with GlobalStore.Lock).  instead call `remote.GetRemoteMap()` (which makes a copy of the map using the lock for protection).  you can then safely iterate over the copy.

This change has nothing to do with my pr, I found a pretty cool concurrency bug

the state returned from this function (`GetRemoteRuntimeState`) gets sent to the update websocket in `NotifyRemoteUpdate` (remote.go:615)
and vars here is pointing to mshell memory, so when it gets marshaled to send to the client in `RunUpdates` (scws.go:129) ->. wsshell `WriteJson` (wsshell.go:75), there is a race condition where any other call to `GetRemoteRuntimeState` will cause the memory in msh.Remote.StateVars to be both written to and read at the same time, even though there is a lock 

I do a deep copy of vars here in order to fix this
I think you're going to need to make a deep copy here to avoid any weird concurrency issues.

i'm going to change this back to passing `nil`.  I think that's the right call here.  This is a new file.  The only way that the flock could block is if someone else is creating this exact same file at the same time.  In that situation it would be better to error immediately (with the EWOULDBLOCK) than to wait the 20ms and get the lock and proceed with the code.
